content:
# Acts Chapter 24 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Chapter:** Acts 24 (Trial before Felix)

| Verse | Issue | EMET | LOGOS | PNEUMA | SOPHIA Resolution |
|-------|-------|------|-------|--------|-------------------|
| 1 | "elders" — article/quantifier | "the elders" (with article) | "some elders" | "some of the elders" | "some elders" — LOGOS reading; the Greek ΤΙΝΩΝ ΠΡΕΣΒΥΤΕΡΩΝ supports "some/certain elders" |
| 1 | "orator" vs "attorney" | "a certain orator, Tertullus" | "a certain orator, Tertullus" | "a professional attorney named Tertullus" | "a certain orator, Tertullus" — EMET/LOGOS; Greek ΡΗΤΟΡΟΣ means "orator/speaker," not specifically "attorney" |
| 1 | Verb for presenting charges | "made-known to the governor against Paul" | "they brought their case against Paul before the governor" | "They filed their case against Paul before the governor" | "they presented their case against Paul before the governor" — combining formal clarity of LOGOS with the legal sense PNEUMA captures; ΕΝΕΦΑΝΙΣΑΝ is a legal term for formal presentation |
| 1 | Location detail | (none) | (none) | "came down to Caesarea" (added) | Omit "to Caesarea" — not explicit in the Greek of this verse; PNEUMA added interpretive context |
| 2 | Opening structure | "And he having been called, Tertullus began to accuse [him], saying" | "And when he had been summoned, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying" | "When Paul was brought in, Tertullus opened his prosecution with flattery" | Follow LOGOS: "And when he had been summoned, Tertullus began to accuse him, saying" — PNEUMA's "brought in" and "opened his prosecution with flattery" are interpretive expansions |
| 2 | Flattery address placement | "Much peace obtaining through you..." (Felix addressed in v.3) | "Since through you we enjoy much peace..." (Felix addressed in v.3) | "Most Excellent Felix, it is thanks to you that we enjoy a long period of peace..." (Felix addressed in v.2) | Follow EMET/LOGOS verse division; Sinaiticus places ΚΡΑΤΙΣΤΕ ΦΗΛΙΞ in v.3 |
| 2 | "peace" phrasing | "Much peace obtaining through you" | "through you we enjoy much peace" | "we enjoy a long period of peace" | "through you we enjoy much peace" — LOGOS balances accuracy and readability; PNEUMA's "long period" is an addition |
| 2 | "forethought" vs "foresight" | "your forethought" | "your foresight" | "your foresight" | "your foresight" — LOGOS/PNEUMA majority; both translate ΠΡΟΝΟΙΑΣ adequately |
| 2 | "reforms coming-about" phrasing | "reforms coming-about to this nation through your forethought" | "reforms are being brought about for this nation by your foresight" | "your foresight has brought reforms to this nation" | Follow LOGOS: "reforms are being brought about for this nation by your foresight" — preserves the passive voice of the Greek |
| 3 | "receive" vs "accept" vs "acknowledge" | "we receive [it]" | "we accept this" | "We acknowledge this" | "we accept this" — LOGOS; ΑΠΟΔΕΧΟΜΕΘΑ means "welcome/accept"; PNEUMA's "acknowledge" shifts the nuance |
| 4 | "hinder" vs "detain" vs "take up time" | "that I might not further hinder you" | "that I may not further detain you" | "not wanting to take up too much of your valuable time" | "that I may not further detain you" — LOGOS; Greek ΕΓΚΟΠΤΩ means "to hinder/detain"; PNEUMA paraphrases too freely |
| 4 | "urge" vs "beg" | "I urge you" | "I beg you" | "I beg your patience" | "I beg you" — LOGOS/PNEUMA; Greek ΠΑΡΑΚΑΛΩ can mean either, but "beg" fits the forensic deference |
| 4 | "clemency" vs "forbearance" vs "patience" | "in your clemency" | "in your forbearance" | "your patience" | "in your forbearance" — LOGOS; Greek ΕΠΙΕΙΚΕΙΑ means "reasonableness/forbearance"; "clemency" (EMET) is slightly too legal, "patience" (PNEUMA) is too general |
| 5 | "pestilence" vs "plague" | "a pestilence" | "a pestilence" | "a plague" | "a pestilence" — EMET/LOGOS majority; both terms translate ΛΟΙΜΟΝ but "pestilence" has stronger biblical register |
| 5 | "moving insurrection" vs "stirs up dissension" vs "stirs up riots" | "moving insurrection among all the Jews" | "one who stirs up dissension among all the Jews" | "a troublemaker who stirs up riots among Jews" | "one who stirs up dissension among all the Jews" — LOGOS; Greek ΚΙΝΟΥΝΤΑ ΣΤΑΣΙΝ means "moving sedition/dissension"; PNEUMA's "riots" is too strong, EMET's "moving insurrection" too literal |
| 5 | "inhabited-earth" vs "world" | "throughout the inhabited-earth" | "throughout the world" | "throughout the entire world" | "throughout the world" — LOGOS; ΟΙΚΟΥΜΕΝΗ means "the inhabited world"; PNEUMA adds "entire" |
| 6 | "profane" vs "desecrate" | "to profane the temple" | "to profane the temple" | "to desecrate the temple" | "to profane the temple" — EMET/LOGOS majority; ΒΕΒΗΛΩΣΑΙ is accurately rendered by both terms but "profane" has stronger biblical register |
| 6 | "seized" — phrasing | "whom also we seized" | "and we seized him" | "so we seized him" | "and we seized him" — LOGOS; cleanest English rendering of the Greek |
| 8 | Phrasing of examination clause | "from whom you yourself having examined [him] will be able concerning all these things to know-fully of what we accuse him" | "From whom you yourself, by examining him, will be able to learn about all these things of which we accuse him" | "You yourself, by examining him, will be able to verify everything we are charging him with" | Follow LOGOS — preserves "from whom" (ΠΑΡ ΟΥ) and "learn" for ΕΠΙΓΝΩΝΑΙ; PNEUMA's "verify" and "charging him with" are interpretive |
| 9 | "joined-in-the-attack" vs "joined in the attack" | "the Jews also joined-in-the-attack" | "the Jews also joined in the attack" | "The Jewish leaders joined in the attack" | "the Jews also joined in the attack" — LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "leaders" not in the Greek; EMET's hyphenation is unnecessary |
| 9 | "asserting" vs "affirming" vs "insisting" | "asserting these things to be so" | "affirming that these things were so" | "insisting that all of this was true" | "affirming that these things were so" — LOGOS; Greek ΦΑΣΚΟΝΤΕΣ means "asserting/affirming"; PNEUMA's "insisting" and "all of this was true" add intensity not in the Greek |
| 10 | Opening structure | "And Paul answered, the governor having nodded to him to speak" | "And when the governor had nodded to him to speak, Paul answered" | "Then the governor nodded to Paul, and Paul spoke up" | Follow LOGOS word order — more natural English while preserving the Greek participial structure |
| 10 | "Knowing [you] to be a judge" phrasing | "Knowing [you] to be a judge to this nation for many years" | "Knowing that for many years you have been a judge to this nation" | "I know that you have been a judge over this nation for many years" | Follow LOGOS — preserves participial "Knowing" (ΕΠΙΣΤΑΜΕΝΟΣ) while being clear; PNEUMA shifts to indicative "I know" |
| 10 | "cheerfully...make-defense" | "cheerfully the [things] concerning myself I make-defense" | "I cheerfully make my defense concerning the things about myself" | "I am glad to make my defense before you" | Follow LOGOS — "I cheerfully make my defense" preserves ΕΥΘΥΜΩΣ and is natural English; PNEUMA's "glad" is acceptable but "before you" replaces "concerning the things about myself" |
| 11 | Opening construction | "You being able to know that..." | "as you are able to ascertain that..." | "You can easily verify that..." | "as you are able to ascertain that" — LOGOS; PNEUMA's "easily" is added; EMET is too literal |
| 11 | "from which I went up" | "from which I went up to worship in Jerusalem" | "since I went up to worship in Jerusalem" | "since I went up to Jerusalem to worship" | "since I went up to worship in Jerusalem" — LOGOS; word order follows Greek ΠΡΟΣΚΥΝΗΣΩΝ ΕΙΣ ΙΕΡΟΥΣΑΛΗΜ |
| 12 | "disputing" vs "arguing" | "disputing with anyone" | "disputing with anyone" | "arguing with anyone" | "disputing with anyone" — EMET/LOGOS; ΔΙΑΛΕΓΟΜΕΝΟΝ is more precisely "disputing/discussing" than "arguing" |
| 12 | "making a gathering of a crowd" vs "stirring up a crowd" | "or making a gathering of a crowd" | "or stirring up a crowd" | "or stirring up a crowd" | "or stirring up a crowd" — LOGOS/PNEUMA; more natural English for ΕΠΙΣΤΑΣΙΝ ΠΟΙΟΥΝΤΑ ΟΧΛΟΥ |
| 13 | Phrasing of inability to prove | "nor are they able to prove to you the [things] concerning which they now accuse me" | "nor are they able to prove to you the things of which they now accuse me" | "they cannot prove a single one of the charges they are now bringing against me" | Follow LOGOS — preserves Greek structure; PNEUMA's "a single one of the charges" and "bringing against me" are interpretive expansions |
| 14 | "confess" vs "tell you openly" | "I confess this to you" | "this I confess to you" | "I will tell you this openly" | "this I confess to you" — LOGOS; Greek ΟΜΟΛΟΓΩ means "confess/acknowledge"; PNEUMA paraphrases |
| 14 | "serve" vs "worship" | "so I serve the ancestral God" | "so I serve the God of our fathers" | "I do worship the God of our ancestors" | "so I serve the God of our fathers" — LOGOS; Greek ΛΑΤΡΕΥΩ can mean "serve/worship" but "serve" is more precise for cultic service; "our fathers" for ΠΑΤΡΩΩ preferred over EMET's "ancestral" or PNEUMA's "our ancestors" |
| 14 | "believing" clause structure | "believing all the [things] according to the law and the [things] having been written in the prophets" | "believing all things that are in accordance with the Law and that are written in the Prophets" | "I believe everything that is written in the Law and in the Prophets" | Follow LOGOS — preserves the two-part structure (things according to the Law AND things written in the Prophets); PNEUMA collapses "written" to cover both Law and Prophets, losing the Greek distinction |
| 15 | "hope toward God" vs "hope in God" vs "same hope" | "having hope toward God" | "having a hope in God" | "I hold the same hope in God" | "having a hope in God" — LOGOS; PNEUMA's "hold the same hope" adds "same" not in the Greek; EMET's "toward" is more literal for ΕΧΩΝ ΕΛΠΙΔΑ ΕΙΣ ΤΟΝ ΘΕΟΝ but "in" is standard English |
| 15 | "await" vs "accept" | "these [men] themselves await" | "these men themselves also await" | "these men themselves accept" | "these men themselves also await" — LOGOS; Greek ΠΡΟΣΔΕΧΟΝΤΑΙ means "await/expect"; PNEUMA's "accept" shifts meaning |
| 15 | "is about to be" vs "is going to be" vs "there will be" | "is about to be a resurrection" | "there is going to be a resurrection" | "there will be a resurrection" | "there is going to be a resurrection" — LOGOS; captures the imminence of ΜΕΛΛΕΙΝ better than PNEUMA's simple future |
| 16 | "exercise [myself]" vs "discipline myself" | "I myself exercise [myself] to have a blameless conscience toward God and men always" | "I also exercise myself to have a blameless conscience toward God and toward men always" | "I discipline myself to keep a clear conscience before God and before people at all times" | Follow LOGOS — "exercise myself" for ΑΣΚΩ is more literal; PNEUMA's "discipline" is acceptable but "clear conscience" for ΑΠΡΟΣΚΟΠΟΝ ΣΥΝΕΙΔΗΣΙΝ loses "blameless"; "people" for ΑΝΘΡΩΠΟΥΣ is too informal |
| 17 | "after many years" vs "after being away for several years" | "after many years" | "after many years" | "After being away for several years" | "after many years" — EMET/LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "being away" and changes "many" to "several" |
| 17 | "came [to] bring" vs "came to bring" vs "came to Jerusalem to bring" | "I came [to] bring alms to my nation and offerings" | "I came to bring alms to my nation and offerings" | "I came to Jerusalem to bring charitable gifts to my people and to present offerings" | "I came to bring alms to my nation and offerings" — LOGOS; PNEUMA adds "to Jerusalem" and changes "alms" to "charitable gifts" and "nation" to "people" |
| 18 | "having been purified" vs "purified" vs "ritually purified" | "they found me having been purified in the temple" | "they found me purified in the temple" | "ritually purified in the temple" | "they found me purified in the temple" — LOGOS; PNEUMA's "ritually" is interpretive addition |
| 18 | Structure of verse | "in which they found me...not with a crowd nor with tumult" | "in the midst of which they found me...not with a crowd nor with a tumult; but there were certain Jews from Asia" | "That is what I was doing...with no crowd and no disturbance — when some Jews from the province of Asia found me" | Follow LOGOS structure — preserves Greek flow; PNEUMA restructures extensively and adds "province of" |
| 19 | Phrasing | "who ought before you to be present and to accuse, if they should have anything against me" | "who ought to be present before you and to bring accusation, if they should have anything against me" | "They are the ones who should be standing here before you, if they have anything against me" | Follow LOGOS — "who ought to be present before you and to bring accusation" preserves the Greek participial connection to v.18; PNEUMA makes a new sentence and drops "to bring accusation" |
| 20 | "wrong-doing" vs "wrongdoing" vs "crime" | "what wrong-doing they found" | "what wrongdoing they found" | "what crime they found" | "what wrongdoing they found" — LOGOS; PNEUMA's "crime" is too strong for ΑΔΙΚΗΜΑ; EMET hyphenates unnecessarily |
| 20 | "Sanhedrin" vs "council" | "I stood before the Sanhedrin" | "I stood before the council" | "I stood before the Sanhedrin" | "the council" — LOGOS; Greek ΣΥΝΕΔΡΙΟΥ is literally "council/Sanhedrin"; both are valid but "council" maintains the translation register |
| 21 | "one voice" vs "one statement" vs "one thing I shouted" | "this one voice which I cried out" | "this one statement which I cried out" | "this one thing I shouted out" | "this one statement which I cried out" — LOGOS; Greek ΦΩΝΗΣ is literally "voice/utterance" but "statement" conveys the sense; PNEUMA's "thing I shouted" is too colloquial |
| 21 | "having stood among them" vs "standing among them" vs "while standing among them" | "having stood among them" | "standing among them" | "while standing among them" | "standing among them" — LOGOS; smoothest English for the Greek participle ΕΣΤΩΣ |
| 22 | Opening and Felix's knowledge | "Felix deferred them, having known more accurately the [things] concerning the Way" | "Felix, having a more accurate knowledge of the things concerning the Way, put them off" | "Felix, who actually understood the Way quite well, adjourned the proceedings" | Follow LOGOS — "having a more accurate knowledge" for ΑΚΡΙΒΕΣΤΕΡΟΝ ΕΙΔΩΣ preserves the comparative; PNEUMA's "actually understood...quite well" and "adjourned the proceedings" are paraphrastic |
| 22 | "determine" vs "decide" | "I will determine the [things] concerning you" | "I will decide your case" | "I will decide your case" | "I will decide your case" — LOGOS/PNEUMA; ΔΙΑΓΝΩΣΟΜΑΙ means "determine/decide"; "decide your case" is natural English |
| 23 | "relaxation" vs "some liberty" vs "considerable freedom" | "to have relaxation" | "to have some liberty" | "to give him considerable freedom" | "to have some liberty" — LOGOS; Greek ΑΝΕΣΙΝ means "relief/relaxation/liberty"; PNEUMA's "considerable" is an interpretive addition |
| 23 | "hinder none of his own to minister" vs "prevent none of his own people from attending" vs "let his friends come and take care of his needs" | "to hinder none of his own to minister to him" | "to prevent none of his own people from attending to him" | "to let his friends come and take care of his needs" | Follow LOGOS — "to prevent none of his own people from attending to him"; PNEUMA's "friends" replaces "his own people" (ΤΩΝ ΙΔΙΩΝ) and "take care of his needs" paraphrases ΥΠΗΡΕΤΕΙΝ |
| 24 | "after certain days" vs "after some days" vs "some days later" | "after certain days" | "after some days" | "Some days later" | "after some days" — LOGOS; most natural English for ΜΕΤΑ ΤΙΝΑΣ ΗΜΕΡΑΣ |
| 24 | "Felix having arrived with Drusilla" vs "Felix came with Drusilla" | "Felix having arrived with Drusilla his own wife, being a Jewess" | "Felix came with Drusilla his own wife, who was a Jewess" | "Felix came with his wife Drusilla, who was Jewish" | "Felix came with Drusilla his own wife, who was a Jewess" — LOGOS; PNEUMA drops "own" (ΙΔΙΑ) and changes "a Jewess" to "Jewish" |
| 24 | "sent-for" vs "sent for" | "he sent-for Paul and heard him" | "sent for Paul and heard him" | "sent for Paul. He listened to him talk about" | "sent for Paul and heard him" — LOGOS; PNEUMA restructures into two sentences and changes "heard" to "listened to him talk about" |
| 25 | "reasoning" vs "reasoned" vs "discussing" | "he reasoning concerning" | "as he reasoned about" | "when Paul started discussing" | "as he reasoned about" — LOGOS; PNEUMA's "started discussing" is too informal and adds "started" |
| 25 | "righteousness" vs "justice" | "righteousness and self-control" | "righteousness and self-control" | "justice, self-control" | "righteousness" — EMET/LOGOS; ΔΙΚΑΙΟΣΥΝΗΣ is "righteousness" in biblical register; PNEUMA's "justice" is a valid but different semantic choice |
| 25 | "the judgment, the [one] being about [to come]" vs "the judgment that is to come" vs "the coming judgment" | "the judgment, the [one] being about [to come]" | "the judgment that is to come" | "the coming judgment" | "the judgment that is to come" — LOGOS; balances literal meaning with readability |
| 25 | Felix's reaction | "Felix having become afraid answered" | "Felix became afraid and answered" | "Felix grew frightened" | "Felix became afraid and answered" — LOGOS; ΕΜΦΟΒΟΣ ΓΕΝΟΜΕΝΟΣ is "having become afraid"; PNEUMA's "grew frightened" is acceptable but drops "answered" |
| 25 | Felix's dismissal | "For the [time] now being, go" | "For the present, go away" | "That's enough for now...You can go" | "For the present, go away" — LOGOS; PNEUMA's "That's enough for now" is a free paraphrase of ΤΟ ΝΥΝ ΕΧΟΝ |
| 25 | "having obtained a season" vs "when I find an opportune time" vs "when I find a convenient time" | "having obtained a season I will summon you" | "when I find an opportune time, I will summon you" | "I'll send for you when I find a convenient time" | "when I find an opportune time, I will summon you" — LOGOS; ΚΑΙΡΟΝ ΜΕΤΑΛΑΒΩΝ is "having obtained a fitting time"; "opportune" conveys this well |
| 26 | "money will be given" vs "money would be given" vs "bribe" | "hoping that money will be given to him by Paul" | "hoping that money would be given to him by Paul" | "hoping Paul would offer him a bribe" | "hoping that money would be given to him by Paul" — LOGOS; preserves the Greek passive construction; PNEUMA's "bribe" is interpretive (Greek says ΧΡΗΜΑΤΑ, "money") |
| 26 | "more frequently sending-for him he was conversing" vs "sent for him more frequently and conversed" vs "kept sending for him and talking with him" | "more frequently sending-for him he was conversing with him" | "he also sent for him more frequently and conversed with him" | "he kept sending for him and talking with him" | Follow LOGOS — natural English rendering; PNEUMA's "kept sending" and "talking" lose the formal register |
| 27 | "two years having been fulfilled" vs "when two years had been completed" vs "Two years went by like this" | "two years having been fulfilled" | "when two years had been completed" | "Two years went by like this" | "when two years had been completed" — LOGOS; ΔΙΕΤΙΑΣ ΠΛΗΡΩΘΕΙΣΗΣ is a genitive absolute; PNEUMA's "went by like this" is a free paraphrase |
| 27 | "wanting to lay-down a favor" vs "wishing to grant a favor" vs "wanted to do...a favor" | "wanting to lay-down a favor to the Jews" | "wishing to grant a favor to the Jews" | "wanted to do the Jewish leaders a favor" | "wishing to grant a favor to the Jews" — LOGOS; ΘΕΛΩΝ ΧΑΡΙΤΑ ΚΑΤΑΘΕΣΘΑΙ is idiomatically "to deposit a favor"; PNEUMA adds "leaders" |
| 27 | "left Paul having been bound" vs "left Paul bound" vs "left Paul in prison" | "left Paul having been bound" | "left Paul bound" | "left Paul in prison" | "left Paul bound" — LOGOS; Greek ΔΕΔΕΜΕΝΟΝ means "bound/in bonds"; PNEUMA's "in prison" interprets rather than translates |

-- SOPHIA
